# Introduction


As a result of their exclusive optical and electronic properties, 1nanomaterials, especially functional nanomaterials, are of great importance in a wide range of applications including effective energy storage, 2sensing, 3nanodevices, 4and catalysis. 5Among different types of catalysis, arti  cial enzyme mimetics have attracted tremendous attention in recent years. 6,7

Peroxidase is one type of redox enzyme that catalyzes decomposition of peroxide and oxidation of some peroxidase substrates. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) is an example of a natural peroxidase enzyme, but it has some fundamental disadvantages that hinder its large-scale applications, such as low stability, a requirement for suitable reaction conditions, and high cost in preparation and puri  cation. In the search for alternatives, comprehensive investigations have been performed using a variety of nanoparticles, functional polymers, and hybrid composites as catalysts. In the literature, the inherent peroxidase activity of numerous nanomaterials,

# Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of mesoporous nickel oxide for selective cysteine sensing †


Chaiti Ray, Soumen Dutta, Sougata Sarkar, Ramkrishna Sahoo, Anindita Roy and Tarasankar Pal*

Mesoporous nickel oxide nanoflowers (NiO NFs) can be easily synthesized by a two-step synthetic procedure based on modified hydrothermal (MHT) treatment of nickel acetate and ethanol amine in water followed by thermal decomposition at350 /C14C for4h. After thermal treatment, the porosity is increased by18% with retention of parental nickel hydroxide size. In this study, for thefirst time, a new catalytic application of NiO NFs has been revealed in terms of peroxidase-like activity where colorless3,3 0 ,5,5 0tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) is oxidized to blue color product in the presence of H2O2at room temperature. Comparative study confirms that mesoporous NiO NFs exhibit superior catalytic activity to the parent analogues, i.e. Ni(OH)2or bulk NiO. This intrinsic peroxidase-like activity from an easily synthesized inorganic nanomaterial provides an alternative to horseradish peroxidase (HRP) enzyme. The lower Michaelis constant ( Km) value indicates that the catalyst NiO NFs bind efficiently to the test substrate, i.e. TMB. Interestingly, the NiO NFs-catalyzed TMB oxidation, i.e. blue color formation, has been found to be selectively and successively inhibited by a variable amount of cysteine among a set of21congeners. Thus our adopted simple, low-cost and novel colorimetric assay stands to be a highly efficient approach for selective detection of cysteine with a limit of detection (LOD) value of /C24 1.1m M using a simple UV-vis spectrophotometer. The proposed method also exhibits outstanding selectivity and accuracy for N -acetyl cysteine (an analogue of cysteine) estimation in real pharmaceutical samples.

including Fe3O4NPs, 6Co -Al ELDH, 7V2O5NWs, 8polymercoated CeO2NPs, 9BiFeO3NPs, 10TiO2NPs, 11FeS and FeSe NPs, 12Co3O4NPs, 13,14bimetallic alloys NPs, 15,16CuS NPs, 17Fe3O4 -Au NCs, 18Pt NTs, 19graphene oxide (GO), 20Au-reduced graphene oxide composite, 21carbon nanodots (C-dots), 22and single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), 23has been established. However, complex reaction strategy and high cost for the reported cases have encouraged further attempts to  nd a new, low-cost, easily synthesized nanomaterial with high peroxidase mimic activity.

Of the various transition metal-based nanomaterials, nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) has received a good deal of attention because of its easy synthesis to obtain porous architecture, which enhances its potential applications in the areas of superconductors24and photocatalysis. 25Moreover, Ni(OH)2is also a preferable precursor for synthesis of NiO, which is a p-type semiconductor. In this work, b -nickel hydroxide nano- owers (Ni(OH)2NFs) were synthesized using a cost-effective, aqueous solution-based hydrothermal process, followed by calcinations to produce mesoporous NiO NFs. Nickel oxide is also an interesting material with varied well-known applications in solar cells, 26devices, 27gas sensors, 28and catalysis. 29Our synthesized NiO nanostructures demonstrate a new catalytic activity as a peroxidase mimetic, where colorless peroxidase substrate3,3 0 ,5,5 0tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) transforms into

its blue color oxidized form in the presence of H2O2. Thus, NiO NFs-driven oxidation of colorless to blue colored product gives a good measure for colorimetric detection of H2O2.

Cysteine (Cys) is a semi-essential amino acid, containing sulfur as an active donor site. It is found in many proteins in the body, and forms a special disul  de bond. 30This disul  de compound is essential in construction of the secondary structure of protein. 31Hence, Cys has vital roles in the human body in protein synthesis, detoxi  cation, and metabolism, and Cys de  ciency causes slow cell growth, liver damage, hair depigmentation, and malabsorption syndromes. 32N -acetyl cysteine (NAC), a well-known derivative of cysteine, is a pharmaceutical drug and nutrition supplement. It is used as a dietary supplement for its antioxidant and liver protecting actions. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), NAC is listed as an essential medicine for the basic health system. Therefore, detection of Cys and NAC are noteworthy for human health.

To date, scenario detection of Cys has been considered to be extremely signi  cant and demanding. Hence, immense effort has been given to detection of Cys by various techniques, including UV-vis spectroscopy, 33,34  uorescence spectroscopy, 35FTIR spectroscopy, 36electrochemical voltammetry, 37high performance liquid chromatography, 38mass spectroscopy, 39and so on. However, in most of the procedures, expensive instrumentation is required and complicated sample preparations are followed, which suggests limited practical applications. Hence, a colorimetric detection process would be desirable for its simplicity and cost-effectiveness and routine analysis. In this work, developed peroxidase-like activity by NiO NFs behaves as a selective sensor towards Cys, with an extension on NAC detection in pharmaceutical samples with limit of detection /C24 1m M.


# Experimental section


Materials and instruments

The related information is presented as S1in the ESI. †

Synthesis of b -Ni(OH)2and NiO NFs. In typical b -nickel hydroxide nano  owers (Ni(OH)2NFs) synthesis, 0.8mL ethanol amine was dripped into4.2mL0.1M nickel acetate (Ni(OAc)2) solution in a screw-capped test tube of25mL capacity to form a blue colored nickel -ethanol amine complex. Then, with a submerged glass slide (2.5cm /C2 0.7cm), the resulting blue colored solution was sealed in the screw-capped test tube and subjected to overnight ( /C24 12h) heating under modi  ed hydrothermal (MHT) 40conditions at110 /C14C. A  er a speci  c time of12h, a uniform greenish-white thin  lm on the glass slide was deposited and the solid  lm was recovered by decanting the clear supernatant solution. Then, the thin  lm was washed several times with water and dried in an air oven for1h at60 /C14C.

To obtain gray-black nickel oxide nano  owers (NiO NFs), the greenish-white precursor was annealed at350 /C14C for4h under ambient conditions in a tube furnace.

Catalytic oxidation of TMB. To study the peroxidase activity of NiO NFs, catalytic oxidation of3,3 0 ,5,5 0tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in acetate buffer was performed in the presence of H2O2. To investigate the catalytic activity of NiO for TMB oxidation, a

25m L TMB solution (0.01M) was diluted by2.5mL acetate buffer solution (0.01M and pH4.0), and was sequentially treated by0.5mg catalyst and100m L H2O2 ( /C24 0.1M). Kinetic analyses with TMB as substrate were carried out by taking a  xed amount of NiO (0.5mg) as catalyst and H2O2 (100m L) with various TMB concentrations (0 -200m M). Similar kinetic analyses with H2O2as substrate were performed using0.5mg catalyst with a  xed amount of TMB (25m L) and different H2O2concentrations (9 -74mM). The Michaelis -Menten parameters were evaluated from a Linewaver -Burk plot of1/ V0vs. 1/ S0.

Detection of cysteine using NiO. At room temperature, the TMB solution (25m M, 0.01M) and cysteine (Cys) in various concentrations (10 -100m M) were added to2.5mL acetate buffer solution (pH4.0). Then, 0.5mg NiO catalyst and100m L H2O2 (0.1M) were added into the mixture. The variation in blue color of the reaction mixture was monitored and quanti  ed spectrophotometrically, which gives a quantitative measure of the Cys concentration. Naked eye detection of Cys concentration is also possible from a color chart or from the spectrophotometric results.

Again, for quantitative colorimetric detection of N -acetyl cysteine (NAC) at room temperature in2.5mL acetate buffer solution (pH4.0), the TMB solution (25m M, 0.01M) and NAC at various concentrations (5 -60m M), 0.5mg NiO catalyst, and H2O2 (100m L, 0.1M) were added and absorbance was measured using a UV-vis spectrophotometer.

All the catalytic experiments were performed four times to investigate reproducibility.


# Results and discussion


In this work, we report synthesis of walnut-like b -Ni(OH)2NFs hydrothermally using Ni(OAc)2and ethanol amine as precursor compounds. NiO NFs were obtained a  er calcination of b -Ni(OH)2NFs at350 /C14Cfor4h. NiO NFs are introduced as a new catalyst for oxidation of TMB and ortho -phenylene diamine (OPDA) in the presence of H2O2 (Scheme1).

Scheme1Schematic representation of stepwise formation of NiO NFs showing peroxidase-like activity toward TMB and OPDA as substrates in the presence of H2O2.

The crystallographic structural characteristics of the asobtained greenish-white b -Ni(OH)2NFs and gray-black NiO NFs were investigated using XRD analysis. The as-synthesized b -Ni(OH)2NFs have single-phase hexagonal structure (Fig. 1a, JCPDS14-0117). The absence of any other peaks authenticates the synthesis of pure b -Ni(OH)2NFs. The gray-black NiO NFs were obtained a  er calcination of b -Ni(OH)2NFs at350 /C14C for4h in air (Fig. 1b, JCPDS71-1179). No peaks were found for b -Ni(OH)2NFs in the XRD pattern of the calcined sample, which con  rms the complete and quantitative transformation of b -Ni(OH)2NFs to NiO NFs.

In the XPS study of Ni(OH)2in Fig. 1b, the presence of Ni2p3/2and Ni2p1/2peaks of Ni(II) at856.2eV and873.9eV indicates the formation of phase pure Ni(OH)2. In addition, the extra two peaks at861.4eV and879.1eV are ascribed as the two satellite peaks of Ni2p3/2and Ni2p3/2, respectively. In Fig. 1c, the XPS spectrum of NiO shows two major peaks at854.03eV and871.8eV for Ni2p3/2and Ni2p1/2of Ni(II), with corresponding satellite peaks at860.1eV and878.4eV, respectively. It is interesting to note that XPS spectra do not contain any other peaks related to Ni(III), which substantiates the formation of pure Ni(OH)2and NiO in respective cases.

FTIR analysis was performed to obtain additional evidence of the as-synthesized b -Ni(OH)2NFs and conversion to NiO NFs a  er calcination (Fig. 1d). The band at459cm /C0 1appeared for Ni -OHstretching frequency, but disappeared in the case of NiO with a new band arising near400cm /C0 1for Ni -O stretching frequency. The presence of a band at /C24 517cm /C0 1 (in plane deformation mode of free hydroxyl groups, d O -H) con  rms the b -phase of Ni(OH)2. At /C24 1380cm /C0 1 , a band appears for the s (NH2), twisted vibration. The peak at /C24 1445cm /C0 1indicates the presence of carbonate ion because of the surface adsorption of atmospheric CO2. The band near1630cm /C0 1is ascribed to stretching and bending vibrations of surface adsorbed (hydrogen bonded) water molecules. The sharp peak at /C24 3647cm /C0 1and a broad peak at /C24 3438cm /C0 1are assigned as a non-

Fig. 1 (a) XRD pattern of (1) b -Ni(OH)2and (2) NiO NFs, (b and c) XPS analysis of b -Ni(OH)2and NiO NFs, respectively, (d) FTIR spectra of b -Ni(OH)2 (black line) and NiO (red line) NFs.

hydrogen bonded free hydroxyl group and a hydrogen bonded hydroxyl group, respectively, in spectrum a. The absences of the peak at /C24 3647cm /C0 1and the comparatively low intense peak at /C24 3438cm /C0 1indicate formation of NiO a  er calcination of b -Ni(OH)2.

The morphology of the light-green colored b -Ni(OH)2NFs was examined using different microscopic techniques. Fig. 2a and b shows corresponding FESEM images at low and medium resolution, which indicate that the architectures have average diameter /C24 3 -5m m and consist of interconnected, smooth surfaced wavy plates of /C24 15nm thickness. The morphology of Ni(OH)2resembles a  ower structure. Thus, that a unique hierarchical, well-de  ned and discrete micro- ower with multilayered texture can be synthesized easily using this facile process is also authenticated by FESEM analysis. Fig. 2c -e shows corresponding TEM, SAED, and HRTEM images of the synthesized Ni(OH)2NFs. In a low-magni  cation TEM image, a single walnut structure was observed (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2d indicates the layered structure of Ni(OH)2NFs. The difference in color shade con  rms the porous nature of the product. From HRTEM analysis the observed lattice gap /C24 0.227nm has good agreement with the d -spacing value of (101) XRD planes (Fig. 2e). In a SAED pattern (inset), bright spots indicate the crystalline nature of the material.

The morphological analyses were performed with the calcined product of b -Ni(OH)2NFs, i.e. NiO NFs. The size of the walnuts almost remains the same, but the interplate spacing is different, which has already been mentioned. This increased spacing increases the porous nature of the material, NiO NFs. From the FESEM images (Fig. 3a and b), the highly porous nature of the NiO NFs can be observed. The TEM analysis was executed to understand the crystallinity and texture of the sample. TEM images (Fig. 3c and d) at medium and low resolution indicate abundantly formed  ower-like structures, organized into distinguishable nanopetals. The darker central part with fading towards the edges reveals the preferential compactness of the nanopetals at the centre rather than the exterior. At the exterior, the difference in contrast of the petal edges and the areas underneath authenticates the porous nature of the sample. In HRTEM analysis (Fig. 3f) the fringe spacing is observed /C24 0.19nm, which indicates the (200) XRD plane, and the SAED pattern shows the highly crystalline nature of NiO NFs.

The porous nature of b -Ni(OH)2NFs and calcined product NiO NFs are evident from FESEM and TEM analyses. Nitrogen adsorption -desorption isotherm measurements were studied to investigate their surface porosity. Fig. 4represents the nitrogen adsorption -desorption isotherms of b -Ni(OH)2and NiO NFs. The calculated Brunauer -Emmett -Teller (BET) surface areas are50.363and113.707m2g /C0 1for b -Ni(OH)2and NiO NFs, respectively. The Barret -Joyner -Halenda (BJH) method is used for measurement of pore diameter distribution of b -Ni(OH)2and NiO NFs (inset of Fig. 4). The pore size for b -Ni(OH)2NFs is3.1nm, which has been increased to3.8nm for NiO NFs.


# Catalysis


In recent years the topic of arti  cial enzymes in biocatalysis has become more popular. In this paper, we demonstrate an

Fig. 2FESEM images of b -Ni(OH)2NFs at low and medium magnification (a and b), TEM images at low and high magnification (c and d), (e) HRTEM image and SAED pattern (inset) of the product.

Fig. 3 (a and b) FESEM images, (c and d) TEM images, (e) HRTEM image and SAED pattern (inset) of NiO NFs.

approach for detection of bioactive molecules based on catalytic activity of a new enzyme-like nanomaterial. Herein, NiO NFs show excellent catalytic property for oxidation of peroxidase substrate, TMB, to produce a blue color in the presence of H2O2. In addition, this peroxidase-like reaction is used for selective and quantitative detection of cysteine.

To investigate the catalytic property of NiO NFs for oxidation of TMB in the presence or absence of H2O2, an oxidation reaction was performed under  ve different conditions: (i) TMB + H2O2 + NiO, (ii) Control1; TMB + H2O2 + Ni(OH)2, (iii) Control2; TMB + H2O2 + bulk NiO, (iv) Control3; TMB + H2O2, (v) Control4; TMB + catalyst. Fig. 5a shows that the Control3system remains colorless, and the Control4shows an insigni  cant

Fig. 4Nitrogen adsorption -desorption isotherm and pore size distribution of (a) b -Ni(OH)2and (b) NiO NFs.

Fig. 5 (a) Adsorption spectra of colorimetric reaction of25m L0.01M TMB with (i) different catalyst (0.003mol mL /C0 1 ) dose, (ii) 100m L0.1M H2O2, (iii) bulk NiO, (iv) Ni(OH)2, (v) NiO (0.003mol mL /C0 1 ) dose + 100m L0.1M H2O2, and (b) absorbance spectra of25m L0.01M TMB in the presence of NiO catalyst (0.003mol mL /C0 1 ) and100m L0.1M H2O2with time.

color change. Again, in the presence of different catalysts, the TMB and H2O2mixture shows blue coloration with different color intensity exhibiting different absorbance values that follow the order: bulk NiO < Ni(OH)2NFs < NiO NFs. These results can be explained in terms of their BET surface area. Synthesized NiO NFs possess more catalytically active surface area than Ni(OH)2NFs as suggested by BET analyses. The catalytic oxidation of TMB by H2O2in the presence of NiO is shown in Fig. 5b.

A leaching experiment was performed to rule out the possibility that the oxidation is caused by leached out Ni(II) ions. To perform the leaching experiment, NiO was incubated in solution for30min at room temperature under ultrasonication. A  er centrifuging, the supernatant was used for the experiment. The insigni  cant absorbance at the spectrophotometer ensured that the peroxidase-like activity initiated from NiO rather than leached out Ni(II) ions(Fig. S1, ESI † ).

Similarly, oxidation of another well-known peroxidase substrate ortho -phenylene diamine (OPDA) was investigated in the presence of synthesized NiO NFs, and the results are shown in Fig. S2 (ESI † ). Within a few minutes, orange coloration occurs with an absorbance peak /C24 443nm in the UV-vis spectra, which is characteristic of OPDA oxidation.

The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of NiO NFs towards TMB oxidation is dependent on pH and H2O2concentration, like other natural enzymes. The catalytic activity was studied with a wide pH range, 3.6 -8.0, and showed maximum activity at pH /C24 4.0 (weak acidic condition) (Fig. 6a). Thus, 10 /C0 2Macetate buffer with a pH of4.0was selected as an optimal reaction condition to achieve higher catalytic activity. The effect of H2O2concentration on the peroxidase process was investigated by varying the concentration in the /C24 10to75mM range (Fig. 6b). Based on peroxidase-like activity of NiO NFs, a colorimetric assay was developed for H2O2detection. Although the absorbance of oxidized TMB is proportional to H2O2concentration, it is a simple way for H2O2detection using a spectrophotometer at l ¼ 653nm. Fig. 6b shows the increase of absorbance of oxidized TMB with increase in H2O2concentration (18.6 -74.5mM). The inset represents a calibration curve of H2O2concentration with a range of18 -56mM, with limit of detection (LOD) 1mM (Fig. 6c).


# Steady-state kinetic analysis of peroxidase substrate oxidation


The peroxidase-like catalytic behavior of the as-synthesized NiO NFs and Ni(OH)2NFs was investigated spectrophotometrically at room temperature upon addition of catalysts into TMB and H2O2mixture separately. The progress of the reaction was examined by following the increase in absorbance value of blue colored oxidized product at l max ¼ 653nm.

2

2

Fig. 6 (a and b) Dependence of peroxidase-like activity of NiO NFs under variable pH and concentration range of H2O2. (c) Linear relation between H2O2concentrations with absorbance.

Furthermore, the kinetics of the oxidation process were investigated applying steady-state kinetics conditions. A series of experiments was performed by varying concentration of one substrate and keeping the other one constant. The absorbance values, i.e. the experimental results, were converted to the corresponding concentration terms using the3value of oxidized blue product (39 000M /C0 1cm /C0 1 ). In a certain concentration range of TMB and H2O2, a typical Michaelis -Menten curve was observed for the NiO catalyst (Fig. 7).

The as-obtained data of the kinetic study were  tted into the Michaelis -Menten model.

All these kinetic parameters are listed in Table S1 (ESI † ). These data are evaluated from the Linewaver -Burk plot (1/ V0vs. 1/ S0).

In both eqn (1) and (2), V0is the initial velocity, S0is the substrate concentration, Km is the Michaelis constant, and Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity.

A  er steady-state kinetic study, it was found that the oxidation process follows the Michaelis -Menten model with NiO and Ni(OH)2NFs as catalysts for both the substrates TMB and H2O2.

The Michaelis constant ( Km) assesses binding affinity between enzyme and substrates. 41The Km value for NiO NFs is0.018mM, lower than the Km value for Ni(OH)2NFs, 0.023mM, and much lower than the Km value for HRP enzyme towards TMB as substrate. The lower value of Km in the case of NiO NFs authenticates the better binding affinity to TMB than Ni(OH)2NFs and HRP enzyme. 42Additionally, the Km values of NiO and Ni(OH)2NFs with H2O2as substrate are1.77and1.76mM, respectively, two times lower than that of HRP, 3.7mM. A comparison of Km values of NiO and Ni(OH)2nanostructures with other reported catalysts is shown in Table1.


# Cysteine detection


In the presence of NiO NFs, oxidation of common peroxidase substrate TMB by H2O2was effectively and successively

suppressed a  er introduction of a trace amount of L-cysteine (L-Cys) solution into the reaction mixture. Following this serendipitous discovery, a method for L-Cys sensing has been proposed. The proposition widens the boundary of NiO catalysis. In Fig. 8a, the typical plot of UV absorbance at l ¼ 653nm versus time (min), shows that an increase in Cys concentration decreases the absorbance value. As a result, the absorbance versus Cys concentration plot in Fig. 8b gives a linear graph within the20 -100m M range ( R2 ¼ 0.9965) with a limit of detection of1.1m M. This estimated LOD value for cysteine is more signi  cant in comparison with the reported procedure using various techniques (Table2).

# Mechanism


L-Cys is a semi-essential amino acid with redox active thiol side chain which is prone to oxidation to give disul  de linking in Cys. Cys has antioxidant properties, and has an important role in restricting the oxidation process of many biomolecules such as tripeptide glutathione. In this work, the presence of a trace amount Cys inhibited the usual H2O2-mediated oxidation of TMB by H2O2. The hindrance in blue color evolution occurs because of thermodynamics-driven preferential oxidation of Cys by H2O2leaving aside TMB. In such a competition Cys is oxidized by H2O2to its disul  de form. 49,50Here, oxidation of Cys by H2O2is more feasible than oxidation of TMB, and this proclamation is explained by following E0values (Table3). Thus, a favorable thermodynamic parameter forces H2O2to  rst oxidize Cys into its disul  de form and oxidation of TMB is inhibited accordingly. This inhibition process is Cys concentration-dependent, i.e. with higher Cys concentration, hindrance of oxidation happens more effectively. Depending on this concentration-dependent inhibiting property, simple sensing of Cys process is manifested.

To investigate the selectivity of this colorimetric assay, we studied the catalytic oxidation of TMB in the presence of other amino acids. Fig. 9shows no color inhibition in the presence of other amino acids and this is because other amino acids do not have any interaction with H2O2like cysteine. Thus, the established sensing system has higher selectivity towards Cys than other amino acids as Cys is vulnerable to oxidation by H2O2in the presence of NiO NFs. Notably, methionine (Met), a sulfurcontaining amino acid, did not signi  cantly inhibit oxidation of TMB. The thiol group in cysteine is more prone to oxidation by H2O2than thioether in methionine. Again, other amino acids having N-type ligand, such as histidine (His) and lysine (Lys), did not show any interaction with H2O2. Even at much higher concentrations of different amino acids, the absorbance remained the same, with color difference from Cys easily noted using the naked eye.

The selectivity and applicability of our sensor for NAC detection were investigated with co-existing compounds in two pharmaceutical samples. The result showed that responses for other additives were absent in colorimetric detection of NAC. A  er addition of a certain concentration of medicine solution in water, an obvious decrease in absorbance was observed compared with the blank test (without any biomolecules). The concentration of NAC in those medicines was calculated from a calibration plot of NAC (Fig. 8c). Results in Table4indicate the similarity of results from the biosensor and those claimed in medicines. For reproducibility, the sensing experiments were performed  ve times under similar conditions and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were calculated. This implies that the sensor is appropriate for reliable determination and analysis of NAC in pharmaceutical drug samples.


# Conclusion


In conclusion, mesoporous NiO NFs, synthesized by a straightforward method, have been shown to exhibit peroxidase-like activity for the  rst time. Thus, the difficulty of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) enzyme manipulation is removed typically. Signi  cant improvement of substrate binding with the catalyst, NiO NFs, is authenticated from the low Km value in comparison with HRP, while we considered a typical Michaelis -Menten model. Again, the NiO catalyzed oxidation of TMB by H2O2was inhibited in the presence of cysteine and also N -acetyl cysteine (NAC). Thus, NiO becomes a biosensor, exhibiting low detection limit, excellent selectivity, and accurate detection of Cys and NAC. This method was successfully applied for NAC detection in pharmaceutical preparations. Overall, according to spectrophotometric measurements, NiO NFs demonstrate outstanding potential application for routine nonenzymatic cysteine sensing.
